2D Gaussian Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Radiance Fields

reasoning: W18 already covered Scaffold-GS lineage; the corrective + sparse-view branch is the freshest material. Pull bodies for Revising Densification, MVSplat, latentSplat, plus 2DGS for the geometry angle.
content: Eight matches. W18 already covered the SDS / LRM lines, so the genuinely fresh material is the corrective and feed-forward-from-sparse-views branch. Reading four bodies.

content.title: Revising Densification in Gaussian Splatting
content.body: Bulò et al. revisit the clone/split/prune schedule from the original 3DGS recipe and show that the criterion that triggers primitive cloning is undertuned. They propose a principled per-primitive criterion based on the variance of position gradients across views, plus a recalibrated opacity-reset schedule, and report a meaningful PSNR gain at equal or lower primitive count on Mip-NeRF 360 and Tanks-and-Temples...

content.title: MVSplat: Efficient 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Multi-View Images
content.body: Chen et al. propose MVSplat, a feed-forward network that consumes a handful of posed multi-view images and directly predicts a set of 3D Gaussians, sidestepping per-scene optimisation. The architecture builds a cost volume on a plane sweep, decodes per-view depth, unprojects each pixel into a Gaussian primitive, and refines them with cross-view attention...

content.title: latentSplat: Autoencoding Variational Gaussians for Fast Generalizable 3D Reconstruction
content.body: Wewer et al. frame sparse-view reconstruction as variational inference over Gaussian parameters. A VAE encoder produces a latent posterior over Gaussian fields conditioned on the input views; sampling and decoding gives a coherent splat field that renders in real time...

content.title: 2D Gaussian Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Radiance Fields
content.body: Huang et al. collapse each 3D volumetric primitive to a flat, oriented 2D Gaussian disk so the geometry implied by the primitive cloud is view-consistent. A perspective-correct rasteriser keeps runtime close to 3DGS, and two regularisers (depth distortion, normal consistency) make the resulting meshes competitive with NeuS-style implicit-surface methods...

neighbors.name: How robust is multi-view consistency under fast camera motion?

reasoning: Good — MVSplat edges back to LGM and the multi-view-consistency question. That's the through-line: feed-forward GS still inherits the same multi-view robustness gap.
content: MVSplat and latentSplat are the headline. Both replace per-scene optimisation with a feed-forward predictor, and both still hit the same multi-view-consistency open question MonoGS does. That's the digest spine.

# Weekly synthesis — 2026-W19

W18 closed on the SDS-vs-feed-forward fork. W19's reading sharpens the
feed-forward side: two of this week's Gaussian Splatting arrivals replace
per-scene optimisation with a single network forward pass over a sparse
view set, and a third paper is the corrective on the original 3DGS
densification schedule that everything downstream still inherits.

## The feed-forward Gaussian branch

[MVSplat](../../papers/arxiv-2403-14627/paper.md) (Chen et al., 2024)
and [latentSplat](../../papers/arxiv-2403-16292/paper.md) (Wewer et al.,
2024) both consume a handful of posed views and directly emit a 3D
Gaussian field. MVSplat uses a plane-sweep cost volume and per-pixel
depth unprojection; latentSplat frames the predictor as a VAE over
Gaussian parameters and samples from the latent posterior. Either way,
the per-scene loop that defined the original
[3DGS](../../papers/arxiv-2308-04079/paper.md) recipe goes away. This is
the same architectural bet [LGM](../../papers/arxiv-2402-05054/paper.md)
made — and the three papers together turn feed-forward GS from a
one-paper experiment into a recognisable family.

## The geometry branch keeps moving

[2D Gaussian Splatting](../../papers/arxiv-2403-17888/paper.md) (Huang
et al., 2024) is the cleanest answer so far to 3DGS's view-inconsistent
surface problem. Collapsing each primitive to an oriented disk makes the
implied geometry view-consistent by construction, and the
perspective-correct rasteriser keeps it near 3DGS speed.

## The correction nobody asked for but everyone needs

[Revising Densification](../../papers/arxiv-2404-06109/paper.md) (Bulò
et al., 2024) is the most quietly useful paper of the week. The clone /
split / prune schedule from the original 3DGS implementation was
undertuned; replacing the gradient-magnitude trigger with a per-primitive
variance-of-gradient criterion and recalibrating opacity reset produces a
meaningful PSNR gain at equal primitive count. Every downstream method —
MVSplat, latentSplat, 2DGS, the SLAM variants — inherits the original
schedule and should be re-evaluated under the revised one.

## The open question that keeps surfacing

The MVSplat neighbours include the
[multi-view-consistency](../../questions/multi-view-consistency.md)
open question. Feed-forward GS doesn't escape it — if anything, it
sharpens it: the predictor is conditioned on a sparse view set, and any
failure of those views to constrain occluded regions or fast-changing
motion shows up immediately as floaters in the output. Per-scene methods
at least optimise away some of that; feed-forward methods bake the gap
into a single forward pass.

## What to read next

For W20: anything that benchmarks revised-densification MVSplat against
LGM and the per-scene 3DGS baseline on a fixed view-count budget, with a
multi-view-consistency stress test rather than the usual benign captures.

content: W19 digest drafted. Anchors six papers (3DGS base + four W19 arrivals + LGM for the cross-reference) and surfaces multi-view-consistency under fast motion as the through-line gap.
